text: My name is Thomas Clancy. I'm the associate chief of surgical oncology at Brigham and Women's Hospital in Boston and a cancer surgeon at Brigham and Women's in Danube Harbor. Today we're going to be talking about a patient who underwent a robotic liver resection for colorectal cancer spread to the liver. The patient had been treated for colorectal cancer and on routine surveillance was found to have an area of spread or metastasis to the right lobe of the liver. Surgical resection was offered as part of her treatment course.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to mobilize the liver, separating it from surrounding scar tissue and attachments. The robotic tools provide enhanced articulation, allowing precise manipulation of the liver's connections to the area behind it.

The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound device to visualize the tumor within the liver, scanning for additional lesions. A harmonic scalpel is employed to score and coagulate a line on the liver surface, marking the area for resection. The scalpel divides the liver tissue, ensuring a margin of healthy liver is maintained around the tumor for complete removal.

The surgeon controls robotic instruments to perform coagulation through the liver, minimizing blood loss and providing gentle retraction. An assistant uses a suction aspirator to manage fluids. The robotic system offers enhanced three-dimensional visualization, allowing precise manipulation and vessel grasping if bleeding occurs.

The surgeon rotates the liver to access and dissect along its posterior border. Clips are visible on the gallbladder structures following its removal, as it was obstructing the resection margin. The dissection continues through the liver to remove the specimen, followed by coagulation to cauterize the liver margin and prevent oozing. A hemostatic agent is occasionally applied to the liver surface.

At the end, total blood loss was less than one hundred cc's and the specimen was removed through a port site or little opening just at the patient's umbilicus or belly button. After a very brief hospital stay, the patient was discharged home and reported that after about five days, she was participating in full activities including driving and had really no pain at all. She's able to resume other cancer treatments without any further delay.
